text: Last class you closed the deal and the sponsor signed off with a handshake.
Today the contract arrives: How do you read the clauses and sign the agreement?

This is the clause which sets out the yearly fee.
The party who signs first must keep a copy.
It is the section that covers exclusivity in Brazil.
I called the director who had signed off on the deal.
Here is the first draft. The fee and the term look fine. Are you happy to sign today?
Not quite. The clause which gives you logo rights in any country is too wide. We only agreed to Brazil. I must ask you to amend it before I sign.
Fair point. We will limit it to Brazil. Is there anything else?
Yes. The part that covers liability puts all the risk on the club. I would like to share the liability fifty-fifty, and to add a termination clause for any breach.
Agreed. We will amend those clauses. The director who signs for us will be here tomorrow.
Perfect. Once the draft is clean and valid, both parties sign before a witness, and the agreement is binding.
This clause is too wide. Could you amend it before we sign?
We need a termination clause for any breach of contract.
Once it is valid, both parties sign before a witness.

Hello again, Doctor Nilo, Sofia here from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last time you closed the deal: you handled the objections, you protected your deal breakers, and the sponsor signed off. Congratulations. But the handshake is not the end. Now comes the contract, the written agreement that turns your spoken deal into law. Today you learn to read it, discuss it, and sign it with confidence. So here is some advice. First, every contract is built from clauses. A clause is one numbered section that covers one topic, the payment, the term, the exclusivity. When you read a clause, describe it clearly. Say, this is the clause which sets out the yearly fee, or, this is the section that covers exclusivity. To do this well, you use defining relative clauses. You use who for people and which or that for things. For example, the party who signs first, or the clause which protects our logo. Second, watch the two sides of every contract. There are rights, the things you are allowed to do, and obligations, the things you must do. A good lawyer reads both. Third, look out for the dangerous words. A breach is when one side breaks the agreement. Liability is the legal responsibility you carry if something goes wrong. And a termination clause says how either party can end the contract. Fourth, if a term is wrong, do not sign. Ask to amend it, that means to change it, before you sign. Finally, when the draft is clean and both sides agree, you sign. Make sure the agreement is valid, not void, and that a witness is present. Remember, Doctor, the negotiation lives in the contract. Read every clause that matters, and sign only when it is right. Good luck.

Let me tell you about the contract that almost went wrong for Corinthians. We had closed the deal, the sponsor was happy, and our lawyers sent over the first draft. Most people would just sign, but I read every clause. The first clause, which set out the yearly fee, was perfect. The second clause, which covered the term, said four seasons, exactly what we agreed. But then I found the problem. There was a clause that gave the sponsor the right to use our logo in any country, with no limit. That was not the deal. For us, exclusivity in Brazil was the point that mattered most. So I called the marketing director, the person who had signed off on the deal, and I said, this clause is too wide. It is the section which gives you global rights, and we only agreed to Brazil. I asked him to amend it before anyone signed. He understood. Then I looked at the liability clause, the part that says who pays if something goes wrong. It put all the risk on us. So I asked to share the liability, fifty fifty. He agreed. Finally, we added a clear termination clause, the section that says how either party can end the contract if there is a breach. Once the draft was clean, we met to sign. I checked that the agreement was valid, that the dates were right, and that a witness was present. Then both parties signed. The lesson is simple. A spoken deal is only a promise. The contract is the law. Read every clause that matters, fix what is wrong, and sign only when it is right. That one afternoon of careful reading protected the club for four seasons.

Once it is valid, both parties sign before a witness.

Here is how to read and sign a contract. First, read the draft clause by clause. Then, describe each clause clearly with who, which or that. After that, find any clause that is wrong and ask the other party to amend it. Next, check that the agreement is valid, not void. Finally, both parties sign before a witness and the contract becomes binding.
